issue.title: Evaluate Python Android framework candidates with explicit selection criteria

comment.body:
> So, you are thinking about something like `Arduino`?
> 
> void setup() {
>     // Initialization code here
> }
> 
> void loop() {
>     // Main loop code here
> }

Yeah, I think that would work. 

> You don't need basic users to think about `abcvlib` interop like this:
> 
> from jp.oist.abcvlib.core.inputs.microcontroller import WheelData
> 
> Instead, we provide a simpler library module:
> 
> from abcvlib import PublisherManager, WheelData
> publisherManager = PublisherManager()
> # Build WheelData instance using the PublisherManager
> wheelData = WheelData.Builder(publisherManager)
> ....

I don't think this would be necessary. This would imply a fair bit of wrapping and documentation, which I'd like to avoid. I think it would be easier to maintain/document if we still refer to the original Kotlin library  if users want to understand the underlying code for whatever reason. In other words, I don't think squashing everything into a single `abcvlib` import is necessary. Keeping it like `from jp.oist.abcvlib.core.inputs.microcontroller import WheelData` would be better both from a maintainability and clearness to the users where the underlying code resides.
